report:
FITNESS GOAL ANALYSIS REPORT
Goal Period: March 2 – April 25, 2026 (55 days)

────────────────────────────────────────

1. GOAL OUTCOME

1 of 2 goals met.

Goal Met: 1 of 2 | Weight: lost 11.9 lbs (11.9 lbs vs 9.3 lbs target) MET | Body Fat: lost 2.68% (end 16.8% vs 15.0% target) MISSED

The weight goal was cleared by 2.6 lbs and reached with more than a week to spare. The body fat target of 15% was not reached — ending at 16.8%, a gap of 1.8 percentage points. Notably, the directional BF% trend was consistent throughout, but the rate of reduction was not fast enough to close that gap in 55 days.

────────────────────────────────────────

2. THE NUMBERS

Start weight: 139.3 lbs
End weight: 127.4 lbs
Weight lost: 11.9 lbs (target was 9.3 lbs — exceeded by 2.6 lbs)

Start BF%: 19.48%
End BF%: 16.8% (7-day rolling avg basis)
BF% lost: 2.68% (target was 4.48 percentage points to reach 15%)

Start lean mass: 112.2 lbs
End lean mass: 106.0 lbs
Lean mass change: -6.2 lbs lost over the period

Average daily calories: 1,401 (target: 1,442 — running 41 cal/day under)
Calorie logging adherence: 89% of days logged (49 of 55 days)
The first 6 days of the goal had no food logging at all.

Average daily protein: 123.1g (target: 135g; floor: 108g)
Hit protein target (135g+): 39% of logged days
Hit protein floor (108g+): 73% of logged days
Failed to meet even the protein floor: 27% of logged days

Total workout sessions: 39 over 55 days
Average sessions per week: 5.0
Average strain score: 18.55 (on a 0–21 scale, this is consistently high)

Sport breakdown:
Running: 39 sessions (present in every workout session — primary modality)
Weightlifting: 32 sessions (logged alongside runs most days)
HIIT: 1 | Walking: 1 | Basketball: 2 | Pilates: 1

────────────────────────────────────────

3. PROJECTION (BODY FAT GOAL MISSED)

BF% reduction rate over 55 days: approximately 0.049 percentage points per day (2.68% over 55 days).

Remaining gap to target: 16.8% − 15.0% = 1.8 percentage points.

At the observed rate: 1.8 / 0.049 ≈ 37 additional days needed, or approximately 5 to 6 more weeks.

However, there are two important caveats. First, fat loss is rarely linear — rate of BF% reduction has been slowing as weight decreases and the deficit becomes harder to sustain. Second, BF% estimates derived from limited weekly datapoints carry meaningful measurement uncertainty, so the true gap to 15% could be somewhat smaller or larger than reported. The projection of 5–6 weeks should be treated as a rough order-of-magnitude estimate, not a precise forecast.

────────────────────────────────────────

4. WHAT DROVE RESULTS

The most decisive period for weight loss was the final three weeks of the goal (roughly April 7 through April 25), during which weight dropped from approximately 131.2 lbs to 127.4 lbs — about 3.8 lbs in 18 days. Looking back 3–7 days at inputs: starting around April 6–7, average daily protein climbed meaningfully. Weekly average protein rose from 131.6g (week of March 30) to 140.1g (week of April 6) and held at 141.6g the following week. Simultaneously, calories stayed controlled in the 1,296–1,410 range. This combination — tighter deficit with higher protein — coincided with the most sustained and consistent weight drop of the entire goal period.

A second notable shift occurred around March 23–25. Weight dropped sharply from 135.1 lbs on March 23 to 131.2 lbs on March 25 — a 3.9 lb drop in two days. The inputs in the 3–5 days prior (March 18–22) show very low average calories (roughly 1,150–1,220 cal/day on most days), a stretch that followed the massive 3,416-calorie spike on March 14. This suggests the March 14 outlier and the subsequent low-calorie correction days created a compressed deficit window that produced rapid (and likely partially water-weight) loss in late March.

Workout consistency was a stable input throughout: 5 sessions per week was maintained every week except the final week (4 sessions), and strain averaged 18.55, indicating genuinely hard training every week. This consistency almost certainly supported the calorie deficit and helped preserve some metabolic rate, though the 6.2 lb lean mass reduction over the period suggests the training stimulus alone was not sufficient to fully offset lean tissue loss under the sustained deficit.

────────────────────────────────────────

5. WHAT HELD YOU BACK

The body fat goal was missed primarily because of two recurring input failures: inconsistent protein and extreme calorie volatility in the first half of the goal.

Protein was the cleaner miss. The target was 135g daily, but it was hit only 39% of the time. More critically, the protein floor of 108g — the minimum to protect lean mass — was missed 27% of logged days. The worst stretches were March 10 (74.6g), March 12 (53.3g), March 15 (65.8g), March 19 (77.1g), and March 24 (68.5g). These low-protein days occurred during or immediately after high-deficit days and likely contributed to the 6.2 lb lean mass reduction over the period. Had lean mass been better preserved, the BF% endpoint would have been lower for the same scale weight.

Calorie volatility in weeks 1–3 was the second structural problem. The range swung from 539 calories (March 24) to 3,416 calories (March 14), with multiple days below 1,000 calories (March 15 at 1,052, March 16 at 962, April 1 at 934, April 22 at 904, April 25 at 467). Sub-1,000 calorie days are almost certainly undereating rather than deliberate aggressive cutting — they correlate with low-protein days and likely represent incomplete logging or highly disrupted eating days rather than a sustained strategy. This inconsistency almost certainly increased lean mass loss and blunted the quality of fat loss even when scale weight moved.

The first 6 days of the goal had zero food logging. Given those days showed strain scores of 17+ and weights were already dropping, this is a meaningful blind spot at the start of the period.

────────────────────────────────────────

6. MEAL PATTERNS

No-log days clustered at the start: March 2–7 had no calorie or protein data despite active workouts and significant weight movement (139.3 to 136.2 lbs). This was the highest-weight period and the one with the least dietary visibility.

Extreme low-calorie days recurred throughout but were especially concentrated in weeks 2–4 (March 10–26). Days under 1,000 calories appeared 6 times across the goal period: March 12 (777), March 15 (1,052), March 16 (962), March 24 (539), April 1 (934), April 22 (904), and April 25 (467). The 467-calorie final day is almost certainly a partial log (the goal ended that day and logging may have stopped midday) but the pattern of sub-1,000 days is real.

High-calorie day clustering: The single largest outlier was March 14 at 3,416 calories — more than double the target. This was followed by a dramatic pendulum swing to 1,052 calories on March 15. Other notable high days: March 8 (1,867), March 13 (1,928), March 21 (1,930), March 22 (2,137), April 4 (2,295), April 18 (1,732), and April 24 (1,927). Most of the over-target days fell on weekends or adjacent to rest days, suggesting a recurring weekend eating pattern, though the data does not definitively confirm this.

Protein and calorie low days tended to co-occur — the same days that had very low calories also had the worst protein numbers. This is consistent with overall undereating on certain days rather than targeted low-carb/low-fat cuts with maintained protein.

The final three weeks (April 6–25) showed the most controlled meal pattern of the entire goal: fewer extreme swings, calories more consistently in the 1,100–1,650 range, and protein more reliably above 120g. This was when body composition improvement accelerated.

────────────────────────────────────────

7. WORKOUT PATTERNS

Consistency was a genuine strength. Forty-nine workout sessions logged over 55 days (note: the sport breakdown totals more than 39 because some sessions logged multiple sport types simultaneously — running and weightlifting were frequently co-logged). The 5-session-per-week average held for 7 of 8 weeks; only the final week dropped to 4 sessions.

Strain averaged 18.55 out of 21, which is on the higher end and indicates hard effort was sustained throughout. Peak strain days included March 27 (23.03), April 4 (21.1), April 10 (24.05), and April 21 (21.11). The April 10 session at 24.05 is particularly notable as the single highest strain day of the period. There were no extended high-strain clusters followed by visible weight or performance drops, suggesting recovery was adequate.

Running was logged on all 39 primary session days, making it the dominant training modality. Weightlifting was co-logged on 32 of those sessions — meaning on roughly 7 session days, only running was logged. The absence of dedicated strength days without a run component may be relevant: paired run-and-lift sessions mean one of the two is likely being done in a pre-fatigued state, which could reduce the effective stimulus for muscle retention.

Rest day distribution: roughly 2 rest days per week, which aligns with the 5-session average. Rest days appear reasonably distributed, with no multi-day rest gaps until the very end of the goal.

HIIT, basketball, pilates, and walking appear only 1–2 times each — these were exceptions, not patterns.

────────────────────────────────────────

8. LESSONS FOR NEXT GOAL

The following are drawn directly from this data, not general advice.

First, protein floor adherence is the highest-leverage lever for the next goal. This goal hit the 108g floor only 73% of days. Given that 6.2 lbs of lean mass was lost over the period — which directly limited the BF% improvement — protecting the floor on at least 90%+ of days should be the primary dietary discipline target for the next block, not just the calorie target.

Second, eliminate sub-1,000 calorie days. Six such days occurred this period. Whether these represent incomplete logging or actual undereating, neither is productive. They correlate with the worst protein days in the dataset. If the calorie target is 1,442, days under 1,000 calories represent either a data quality failure (incomplete logging) or a dietary pattern failure (crash-recovery cycling). Either way, closing this gap would both improve lean mass retention and make the calorie average more meaningful.

Third, log from day one. The first 6 days of this goal were unlogged despite active training and measurable weight flux. The next goal should treat day-one logging as non-negotiable.

Fourth, the March 14 outlier (3,416 calories) was followed by a visible weight spike and multiple low-calorie correction days. One single event created a ripple that disrupted the dietary pattern for nearly a week. The data suggests that high-calorie days in this range, even occasional ones, have outsized downstream costs in terms of compensatory restriction. Keeping outlier days closer to 1,800–2,000 calories rather than 3,400+ would be a more controllable strategy.

Fifth, the final three weeks of this goal were the most effective — lower calorie volatility, higher protein, consistent training. That three-week input profile (avg ~1,296–1,410 cal, avg ~131–142g protein, 4–5 sessions/week) represents the best demonstrated execution of this plan and should be the baseline template for the next goal rather than the average of the full 55-day period.

Sixth, consider separating at least some strength sessions from runs. Weightlifting was co-logged with running on 32 of 39 session days. Given the 6.2 lb lean mass loss, testing whether standalone lifting sessions (not paired with a run the same day) leads to better lean mass retention is worth examining in the next block.

────────────────────────────────────────
